record_office_terminology.probate: District Court (probate jurisdiction); historically Probate Court (1852-1896)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (county-level from territorial era; state registration from 1887, comprehensive from 1905)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1905 (births and deaths statewide registration; LDS ward records serve as de facto vital records from 1847)

burned_county_authority: Utah has no officially designated 'burned counties.' The Utah State Archives in Salt Lake City is the central repository. FamilySearch (headquartered in Salt Lake City) holds extensive microfilmed copies of county records. Some counties experienced record thinning due to remote location and sparse population rather than catastrophic loss.
administrative_notes: Utah Territory was organized in 1850 from the provisional State of Deseret (1849-1850). Utah was admitted as the 45th state on January 4, 1896, after prolonged conflict over polygamy. The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) dominated Utah's settlement and governance. LDS ward records function as de facto vital records for most of Utah's history (1847-1905). The Family History Library in Salt Lake City is the world's largest genealogical library. County Recorders maintain deed records; County Clerks handle marriage licenses; District Courts have probate jurisdiction. The 29 counties were formed between 1850 and 1917.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Utah has no burned counties in the traditional sense. Some remote counties (Daggett, Wayne, Piute, Rich) have thin records due to sparse population, and Sevier County experienced some record damage during Black Hawk War evacuations (1866-1871), but no Utah county suffered catastrophic courthouse fires destroying records. The Utah State Archives and FamilySearch both maintain backup copies of most county records.

core_repositories.source_name: Utah State Archives and Records Service
core_repositories.repository: Utah Department of Cultural and Community Engagement, Salt Lake City
core_repositories.description: Central state repository for Utah government records. Houses territorial and state government records, county records (microfilm and original), vital records indexes, military records, prison records, mining records, and corporate records. The archives also hold court records, tax assessments, and naturalization records.

core_repositories.repository: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, Salt Lake City
core_repositories.description: The world's largest genealogical library, located in Salt Lake City. Houses over 2.4 million rolls of microfilmed records, 727,000 microfiche, and extensive digital collections from more than 100 countries. Free access to FamilySearch, Ancestry, FindMyPast, and other databases. Essential for Utah and worldwide genealogical research.

core_repositories.repository: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, Salt Lake City
core_repositories.description: Houses the official records of the LDS Church including ward and branch records, membership records, patriarchal blessings, missionary records, immigration records, Perpetual Emigrating Fund records, and pioneer journals. Essential for Utah genealogy — ward records function as de facto vital records from 1847.

Holds federal records for Utah including General Land Office (GLO) homestead entries, Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Uintah and Ouray Reservation, federal court records (naturalization, polygamy prosecutions), Bureau of Reclamation records, and military post records for Camp Floyd, Fort Douglas, and others.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive microfilmed and digitized Utah county records including deeds, marriages, probate, court records, and vital records. Also includes LDS ward records, temple records, membership records, and emigration records. FamilySearch is headquartered in Salt Lake City and has the most comprehensive coverage of Utah records of any online platform.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability charts, links to digitized microfilm collections, and guidance on using LDS church records for genealogical research in Utah.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: J. Willard Marriott Library, University of Utah
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Extensive collection of digitized Utah newspapers including the Deseret News (1850+), Salt Lake Tribune (1871+), and county newspapers from across the state. Free full-text search. One of the best state newspaper digitization projects in the nation.
Additional digitized Utah newspaper titles through the National Digital Newspaper Program. Includes territorial-era papers.

military_records.repository: Utah State Archives / LDS Church History Library
military_records.description: The Nauvoo Legion (Utah territorial militia) records document service in the Walker War (1853-54), Black Hawk War (1865-1872), and Utah War (1857-58). Muster rolls, correspondence, and claims for compensation name participants and their families.

Fort Douglas (established 1862 in Salt Lake City) was the primary federal military installation in Utah. Post returns, muster rolls, and correspondence at NARA document soldiers and their families. Fort Douglas Military Museum at the University of Utah has additional materials.

land_and_property.source_name: Bureau of Land Management — GLO Records
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Utah including homestead, preemption, desert land, mining, and timber culture entries. Utah was public domain — GLO records document original federal-to-private land transfers. Searchable database covers all federal land transactions in Utah.

land_and_property.source_name: Utah County Recorder Offices — Deed Records
land_and_property.description: County Recorders maintain grantor/grantee deed indexes and deed books from county formation. In-person access at each county recorder's office. Some counties have online search portals. Records cover deeds, mortgages, deeds of trust, mining claims, and water rights filings.

vital_records.source_name: Utah Office of Vital Records and Statistics
vital_records.repository: Utah Department of Health and Human Services
vital_records.description: State registration of births and deaths from 1905. Pre-1905 vital events found primarily in LDS ward records (1847+), cemetery records, and county records. Marriage records at county level from territorial era; state registration from 1887 (territorial), comprehensive from 1905.

vital_records.source_name: LDS Ward Records as Vital Records
vital_records.description: LDS ward records are the PRIMARY source for vital events in Utah before 1905. They include: blessing records (births), baptisms (age 8), marriages/sealings, deaths, and membership transfers. Nearly comprehensive for the LDS population which was 90%+ of Utah's population through the 19th century.

church_and_cemetery.description: Temple records from St. George (1877), Logan (1884), Manti (1888), and Salt Lake (1893) temples document endowments, sealings, and baptisms for the dead. These records link living and deceased family members and are available on FamilySearch.

Cemetery transcriptions compiled by DAR, DUP (Daughters of Utah Pioneers), county historical societies, and WPA workers. BillionGraves (headquartered in Utah) has strong Utah coverage with GPS-located headstone photographs. Utah State Archives holds burial records and sexton records for many cemeteries.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes Crossroads (journal). Hosts annual conferences. Provides research resources and education for Utah family history research.

research_guides_and_societies.repository: Daughters of Utah Pioneers, Salt Lake City and county museums
research_guides_and_societies.description: DUP maintains museums in every Utah county with pioneer family files, journals, photographs, and artifacts. The Pioneer Memorial Museum in Salt Lake City is the central repository. DUP publications include county heritage histories and individual family sketches. Essential for pre-1900 Utah genealogy.
Free genealogy resources organized by county. Includes transcribed records, cemetery readings, family histories, and links to county-specific resources.

historical_counties_extinct.source_name: Extinct Utah Counties and Boundary Changes
historical_counties_extinct.description: Several Utah counties were created and later abolished or renamed: Cedar County (1856-1862, absorbed into Iron), Carson County (1854-1861, became part of Nevada Territory), Greasewood County (1856-1862, absorbed into Box Elder), Rio Virgin County (1869-1872, merged into Washington and Kane), Shambip County (1856-1862, absorbed into Tooele and Juab), St. Mary's County (1856-1862, absorbed into Morgan). The provisional State of Deseret (1849-50) also created counties not carried forward into Utah Territory. Green River County (1852-1862) was split into Wasatch, Summit, and Uintah. Researchers must trace these boundary changes to find records filed under predecessor county names.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Uintah and Ouray Agency
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Bureau of Indian Affairs records for the Uintah and Ouray Reservation in Duchesne and Uintah counties. Includes census rolls, allotment records, annuity payment records, school records (Uintah Boarding School, Whiterocks), agency correspondence, and individual Indian case files. The reservation was created for the Ute bands in 1861-1882. The 1905 opening allocated lands and displaced Ute families.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Navajo Agency Records — Utah Navajo (San Juan County)
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Navajo Nation lands extend into San Juan County, Utah. BIA Navajo Agency records at NARA include census rolls, allotment records, and school records for Utah Navajo communities. Navajo chapter house records document local governance. The Navajo Nation has its own record-keeping system separate from federal records.

Records for the Goshute (Skull Valley and Deep Creek Reservations in Tooele and Juab counties) and Paiute (scattered bands in Iron, Millard, Sevier, and Washington counties) peoples. BIA records include census rolls, agency correspondence, and trust land records. The Paiute Indian Tribe of Utah (PITU) was terminated in 1954 and restored in 1980 — termination-era records at NARA document the process.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Northwestern Band of the Shoshone Nation Records
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Northwestern Band of the Shoshone have lands in Box Elder County and are connected to the Bear River Massacre site (1863). BIA records and tribal records document members. The band received federal recognition in 1987. Historical records at NARA Denver and the Utah State Archives.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Mining Immigrant Communities — Greek, Italian, Japanese, Slavic
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Utah State Archives / County Clerks / Ethnic community organizations
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Utah's mining and railroad industries attracted diverse immigrant populations, concentrated in Carbon County (Helper, Price — Greek, Italian, Japanese, Finnish), Salt Lake County (Bingham Canyon — Greek, Italian, Serbian, Croatian, Japanese), and Summit County (Park City — Cornish, Irish, Italian). Records include naturalization papers (filed with district courts), ethnic fraternal organization records, church records (Greek Orthodox, Catholic, Buddhist), mine employment rolls, and ethnic newspaper archives.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: LDS Emigration Records — Perpetual Emigrating Fund
immigration_and_naturalization.description: The Perpetual Emigrating Fund (1849-1887) financed the immigration of thousands of LDS converts from Europe to Utah. Records include ship manifests, wagon train rosters, handcart company records, fund account books naming individuals and families, and mission records documenting converts before emigration. Essential for tracing the origins of most early Utah families.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Denver / Utah State Archives / County District Courts
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from Utah territorial and federal courts. District court records (filed locally) include declarations of intent and petitions for naturalization. Federal court naturalization records at NARA Denver. Utah State Archives holds some transferred county naturalization records. Important for mining-era immigrants (1880s-1920s).
